draft.introduction: The Birds of Aristophanes (414 BCE) imagines two Athenians who convince the birds to build a city in the sky, Cloudcuckooland, blocking the gods’ sacrificial smoke and forcing them to negotiate. It is a comic theosis—mortals ascending to challenge the divine order.

translation.text: Come now, you mortals living in darkness, you creatures of a day, you powerless, fleeting beings, resembling a dream, you wingless ephemera, hear us, the immortal birds, the ever-living, the dwellers in air, the ageless, the thinkers of imperishable thoughts. Learn from us all about heavenly things, the nature of birds and the origin of gods and rivers and chaos and darkness, and after this tell Prodicus from me to go hang himself.
translation.original_text: ἄγε δὴ φύσει σκοτεινοί, φύλλων γενεᾷ προσόμοιοι

translation.text: Hail, hail the victor king! O greatest of the gods! He surpasses even Zeus in power, this ruler of Cloudcuckooland! The thunderbolt of lightning-flashing Zeus is now his to wield!
translation.original_text: ὦ μέγιστε καὶ μακάριε βασιλεῦ τῶν θεῶν

translator_notes.note: The birds’ parabasis parodies cosmogonic poetry (Hesiod, Orphic hymns) while genuinely engaging with pre-Socratic thought about the origin of the cosmos. The line about Prodicus (a sophist who gave naturalistic explanations of the gods) shows Aristophanes simultaneously mocking both traditional religion and its rationalist critics.
